commentBody: Very little resistance for a Saturday, but enjoyable entries
Yeah to Bluey! and The Kiss, gave up on the rest of the trivia.

Yesterday we had people reporting their fastest Friday time. Now I expect we will have people reporting their fastest Saturday time. I hope the record-setters are pleased, but I'd really like a TERM LIMIT for easy themeless puzzles.

(If you don't like this WRITE UP, EXCUSE ME.)

I thought this one was tough and had many fun clues.  My favorite was [What might add a bit of flavor to salsa?] for BONGO.  That misdirection took me some time to suss out.  

Thanks again to my grandson (who is coming from Glasgow for a visit next week) for being responsible for me knowing BLUEY and MARIO.  Engaging with his interests has helped me with the puzzle on numerous occasions.

No thanks to my legal training, which caused me to enter inre rather than ASTO for regarding.  That misstep caused me some delay in sniffing out another legal clue: AFORTIORI.
For me the puzzle was not too hard, not too easy, just right!
Go stuck on Tospare. Never heard of that word or is it two words?

I particularly like BERT crossing BERT at 9 down and 26 across. 

And THEKISS crossing EROTIC.  

This one went very fast for me...   except for a different crossing, which was a major STRIKEOUT for me.  The crossing in 3 down and 13 across. I just did not know either of these. 

And it's okay!  I'm not going to get all BLUEY about it. I don't know everything yet!  Plus, there's a good chance I'll remember OXFAM if it comes up again, probably not the legalese. 

Cheers to the weekend!

I thought this was a throwback tough Saturday for me. First time since in along while I actually had to step away from the puzzle for a few minutes to clear my head. And it first to show it’s not just the grid that makes the difficulty, it’s the cluing, I found them soon and ambiguous (in a good way).

SW fell first for me, the NE, then SE, and finally the NW which is where I had to clear my head. Ever had a simple entry that just keep evading your neurons? That was SCORE for me. I had REI thanks to my daughter, who works for them; but I was stuck with INRE before ASTO and PAPIST before PURIST, which made perfect sense since ___PRIORI seemed to be likely candidate for the legal term. But nothing was falling into place. Was trying to find another nickname for a building superintendent.

Nothing too flashy, but I thought a solidly clued Saturday with a mix of some entires just onthe edge of my knowledge base (JOJOBAOIL, DIEGOLUNA,THEKISS) some fun entries (IJUSTWORKHERE, EMOJIKEYBOARD) some misdirectionsclues like HOARDED

Once again, early returns are that this was a breeze.

I'm quite disappointed because it took me over an hour, which is about what Friday/Saturday used to take me before all the talk about the Great Dumbing Down.

I don't know, I may be going back to the side of those who don't mind the new puzzles.  It almost seems like the trend now is to declare all puzzles unfit by NYT standards.

For example:  5A.  Is that ORC or ELF or ENT?  And 21A is SIS?  Really?  Doesn't seem to me to fit very well.  

QBERT is a new one for me, as almost all gaming terms are.  Same with OXFAM--I'd heard of them, barely, but I had no idea they had a relationship to Nairobi.

I see now how ROD is a [Line Holder], but that seem to me to be a Saturday worthy clue.  Same with GEM for [Brilliant Example].  

I also had no idea the SITAR was held at a 45 degree angle to the body.  For me it was a matter of coming up with any instrument that is held and has five letters.

I'm not saying this puzzle was extraordinarily difficult, but I really didn't think this was a walk in the park.
  
JOJOBA OIL?  That was entirely unsolvable for me without every single cross in the first six letters.

I guess my question is this:  if you are smart enough to think this puzzle is easy, then why don't we have an answer to why only odd numbers of socks come out of a dryer.
I came to say how much I love the clue and answer to 37 across!

"You got me a keyboard for our anniversary? You're just my type!"

(Yeah, I need a new keyboard too - just put it on my tab.)

I still have a TIVO 🤭 so I struggled with that one. Not bygone yet!

Also caught on CREamEGG. I'm a gardener but somehow I never heard of GRUBS 🤷🏼‍♀️ (also didn't know that claw thing is called a cultivator which I learned on Jeopardy yesterday)

For me there were enough unknowns, puns and question marks to give a worthy and fun struggle - over my average time. Thanks Ryan!

Speaking of TERMLIMITs, I'm just curious about what the founding fathers would think about so many of our federal senators having lifetime appointments in the Senate.

I wonder if the writers of our Constitution thought about that, spoke about that or wrote about that.

And is it a good thing or a bad thing? I wonder how Lindsay Graham's sister will fit into the whole plan.

When I saw they long down entry in the northwest for a legal term. I figured I was cooked for that section of the grid.

But it turned out to be the southeast that gave me the most trouble. note to self. It is crème egg not cream egg that little error took over 15 minutes to decode, not pleased, but on me, I guess.

But to make it a full circle soft my fly spec brought me back to the Northwest where the cross of a legal term and an African based NGO made me play the alphabet game. 

I don’t feel it was hard overall. The Q and the Js in JOJOBA really helped with crosses.  but again,  when you mistake cream for crème, it’s gonna be a long solve. At least it was for me. 

I guess clue word of the day was titular. 

Nice Saturday.

Oof, that SE corner. My own fault, of course, for somehow being stuck on frozen eggs and not having EMBRYOS come to mind (duh), then knowing nothing about either TV show. Both really slowed me down. Oh, and being stuck on CREam EGG. I had to close the puzzle, do Connections and Wordle, and wait for the prefrontal cortex to do its thing in the meantime. 

6:18am. Still haven’t had my coffee as I’m trying to ignore the persistent meow-meooOOWW from next to the bed. God forbid I be allowed to sleep in on a Saturday. Señor Gato does not observe weekends. 

I so envy my husband’s selective deafness and the ability to withstand The Unblinking Stare which follows the vocal demands.

Puzzle with 30A released online on July 17, World Emoji Day. 

Coincidence?

That one was so far out of reach I just gave up at a certain point and started revealing things. My worst Saturday showing in some time. Different wavelengths and all that. I took a hard bike ride in extreme heat today so maybe I just melted down my neurons or something.

I managed to tease out all the maddening entries—JOJOBA, WIDEBERTH, GRUBS—you know the ones I'm talking about, and was feeling pretty cocky.  Then I went back to the NW corner, which I'd had to skip, and I was still totally stymied.  Latin legal terms are not on my boat, let alone in my wheelhouse, and I had to come to the column for help.   

It was a worthy Saturday workout, and I'm not complaining about the difficulty, but c'mon, AFORTIORI? It's even underlined in red here—they never heard of it either.

Thanks, Ryan. I really enjoyed the part I enjoyed, and I don't mean to be judgmental, but my agéd brow just got agéd-er.  See you next time. I'll be ready for you.

BONGO excellent 😀

A fair Saturday. Trepidation at first but with a foothold it all game together quite nicely and in decent time. 

Didn’t know the trivia but deduction is part of the game.

For some reason I got confused in the SE corner a d needed to consult the column to resolve the crossing of TAL_N and B_NGO - probably because I'm only very vaguely aware of bongo drums and their connection to salsa.

Other than that I solved the puzzle on my own.

So this was the day when being a lawyer finally paid off! Polish and American legal lingos are different, but A FORTIORI was (almost) a gimme: I got it with a minimum of crosses. Seriously though, that's not a fair entry, even on Saturday!

There was pretty much no advertising in Poland before 1989. When commercials began appearing after the introduction of a market economy, especially on TV, they were a sensation. For a year or two everybody watched them as if they were a show. We quoted the lines and slogans at one another, and some of them I remember 35 years later. That's how I knew JOJOBA OIL - it was mentioned in a shampoo commercial. These days I can't stand advertising of any sort - it's so cool there's none of it on streaming platforms! Commercial breaks are the main reason I haven't watched linear TV for a decade or more.

I didn't like the clue for TOMB, even though I got it without much troubke: to me, a graveyard might be described as a dead center, but a tomb? There is misdirection, and then there are stretches such as this 🤷🏽

Some clues I don't get: "Digs up by the roots": GRUBS. Maybe I'm just not familiar with some meanings of the word "grub"? And how are RODS "line holders"?

Today was not in my Saturday wheelhouse. I tend to switch off on weekends but had enough neurons to spare to crack a few and leave a wide berth for others. Did realise I have to take potential solves to a third or fourth option like serve restriction - I had alcohol and tennis but didn't press any further. I enjoyed the challenge even though I missed Bluey! Very un-Aussie of me - 'struth!

Greek version of presumably emued post:

Solid post-Easening Saturday. (Yes, I am making lemonade today instead of just sμcκιηg on them. And yes, Andrzej, if the emus allow this formulation, I wrote "just sμcκιηg” on them.") 

Was initially mad at HOARDED, which I got only on crosses and even then didn't get at all until I got it and grinned. 

Dang if I haven't sung "back room voice" every time I've belted out LOLA these past 45 years or so. Thanks, Ryan. 

TOSPARE laconically brilliant as clued.

Nice bit of word play here and there.
That yoga mislead SITUPS (HA!) had me tied up like a pretzel.
Guessing lotza solvers sat up and took notice.
Not to mention the opening legal thriller in the top corner.

Long live the work ethic and creative drive that keeps constructors going. You never know where you're next great challenge is coming from. 

As for me, 📺🍿🛋️

I will never be even close to one of the best at these. There have been times where I thought and had accumulated evidence that I was getting pretty good. This was very difficult for me. Almost an hour and that’s fine with me. I loved it. And, I guess there’s times where I’m not on my game. I’ll go with that. Thanks to whoever put this one together. I got it with absolutely zero assistance.
Enjoyable puzzle, thanks

Hey Ryan Judge - You really got me!

Was planning to sing your praises at the interval, when I saw these lines cascading from the podium as the conductor rushed off the stage . . .

Dirty eraser, must you keep rubbing
Plowing into my time?
Tivos so bluey, make me feel dizzy
Erotic light shines so bright
But I don't need no friends
As long as I bail with
Emoji keyboard
I am in paradise

Every day, I stare at the time on my crossword
But silly, silly is the forum tomb
Emoji keyboard time (🧑⌨️🕒 )

Lola meets Mario
Emoji keyboard
Is it 💋💋 tonight?
But a fortiori, don't want to strike out
So I 📺🍿🛋️ tonight
But I don't feel afraid
As long as I write up
Emoji keyboard
I am in paradise

(excuse me, just working out the kinks)
Cmon oxfam crossing a fortioi is ridiculous. Rest of puzzle was fine. No idea who is Diego Luna but it can be gotten from crosses.

Solid post-Easening Saturday.  (Yes, I am making lemonade today instead of just sucking on them.  And yes, Andrzej, if the emus allow this formulation, I wrote "just sucking on them.")

Was initially mad at HOARDED, which I got only on crosses and even then didn't get at all until I got it and grinned.

Dang if I haven't sung "back room voice" every time I've belted out LOLA these past 45 years or so.  Thanks, Ryan.

TOSPARE laconically brilliant as clued.
Diego Luna - also a soccer player in MLS and one of the last cuts from the USMNT World Cup roster. Next time, Ryan? Tricky puzzle, no idea what FAE is, got that and FIT for mesh last.

One of the best things about Andor — perhaps the very best thing for language lovers — was its Ghorman language, a kind of mock-French that sounded like French but wasn’t French. It’s hilarious and brilliant and outrageously faux, and the icing on the cake for one of the very best things in the entire Star Wars universe. Here’s a taste of Ghorman (no spoilers): https://share.google/zTtUAytiGTiEMhdkI
Watching a show I recorded on my TIVO while I solve this puzzle. The reports of its death are greatly exaggerated.

Couldn't finish this until the morning. The northwest corner was too much.  

1D Left as a clue could've been anything.      So once that got figured out, it was fine.    Agree that oxfam/afortiori cross was nasty.   As was Diego Luna/bluey

That was a nice workout. Very chewy in places; looking at you A FORTIORI. Definitely had to go with a look up on that one. The rest fell slowly, helped by the fact that youngest son was in the room to give me the actor’s name and between us EMOJI BOARD. He introduced me to Andor, which I absolutely loved, but would never have remembered DIEGO’S name on my own.

I stuck with net for FIT for too long, which held that area up til the end. What supernatural race begins with N? Doh.

It seems OXFAM isn’t so well known out of the UK then. They do fabulous dedicated second hand book stores. I can spend hours in there and come out with armfuls of tomes.

OT. Still not crazy about the new comments set up. It’s a tiny page within a page on my pad that’s not easy to read or comment on. Plus I have to upload the ‘cheat’ grid at the bottom of Wordplay if I want to refer back to the puzzle, rather than my completed grid. All in all it’s a clunky faff of an app. There was nothing wrong with the old system. Stop messing about chaps, ‘if it’s not broke, don’t fix it’.

Oh, it’s that sweet Saturday combination – a box awash in lovely answers that you have to conquer barriers to get to.

It makes the brain happy; it makes the aesthetic sensibilities happy.  A GEM of an answer set infused with SLY cluing.

Some of those lovely answers: 
•	Top tier – PURIST, WIDE BERTH, LETS FLY, I JUST WORK HERE.
•	Not far behind – STUDY DATE, HEFT, BONGO, TO SPARE, THE KISS.

Many answers that required crosses to make the clue click, i.e., [Left] for TO SPARE and [Line holders] for RODS.  But when the clue clicked, it was very sweet, and what ensued was a staccato of happy-pings that lit up the box like a Christmas tree.

More pings came from word-playing riddle clues – such as [Dead center?] for TOMB, and [Overtook?] for HOARDED.

My kind of Saturday – work and the FRUIT of work.  I left the puzzle with a lit-up serene brain, and that’s a gift.  High respect for what you did here, Ryan, and much gratitude.  More please!

Other than the F in OXFAM,
this one just flew by so fast
I could hardly get a good look at it!

So I *did* look back and loved

I JUST WORK HERE
WIDE BERTH
EXCUSE ME
THE KISS
EMOJI KEYBOARD…

May I please, once again,
plead to the Deaf Ears Council

for a Crunchy Clue option?
Even just for Saturdays?

I suggested early last night that after the "fastest Friday" comments yesterday we might see some "fastest Saturday" comments today. Not from everyone, but from some. This morning, I see "45% solved much faster (>20%) than their Saturday average." https://xwstats.com/puzzles/2026-07-18

In ancient Greece, I believe there was a really long race of supernatural spirits: the ultrafae.


It’s Saturday: let’s fly.

👏🏼👏🏼👏🏼 Now that’s a proper Saturday challenge. I started this last night and had to set it down until this morning. That NW corner almost did me in! I had never heard of OXFAM and I thought maybe the legal term might end in PRIORI 🤷🏼‍♀️ but alas, no. I swapped ‘in re’ and AS TO at least three times before settling on the correct answer. I also had vIola 🎻 before SITAR, but EGAN cleared that up once I went through the across clues again. 

Arrived in Wisconsin last night. As we were driving, a tick fell on my daughter in our rental car. She felt it on her arm thinking a piece of hair was tickling her. She flicked it off and realized quickly that it wasn’t hair 😱 Luckily we were almost to the house where we are staying. Our host graciously removed said tick from the vehicle 🫣 *shudder* I sent a picture of it to our family chat and everyone freaked out. Nasty looking critter! Both my son and his girlfriend have been bit by ticks. He caught his in time by recognizing the telltale bullseye 🎯, but his girlfriend was less lucky and contracted Lyme disease 🫩 Horrible disease. 

Anyhoo, thanks , Ryan, for this challenge. I loved “I JUST WORK HERE” 🤣
Loved the Moe Berg reference because I read the book "The Catcher Was A Spy" many years ago.
Ryan is a master of the modern puzzle in which phrases dominate.  Some are very familiar, a few are idiosyncratic stretches. His puzzles are the most enjoyable of all, for me.

Since Barry just posted the link to xwstats for today, I won’t bother, but I’ll point out that it went from Easy last night to Average this morning. That tracks, because most of the early returns are experienced solvers, whose experience comes very much into play today. 

I won’t complain that this one was too hard, so the naysayers can’t say we always claim the day’s puzzle isn’t like those of old. On the contrary, this one displays many of the traits of the pre-Easening puzzles. 

Some examples:

Let’s start at the beginning. 1A could just as easily been MEET, and 1D was a very vague clue. 3D was an obscure Latin legal term, but could be sussed out because of the root “fort” and similarities to “a priori” and the legal term “certiorari.”  13A was a charity that we sometimes hear of in the US, but not nearly as much as in other places, like the UK, where their shops are the equivalent of our Goodwill shops. 

Experienced solvers who have never seen “Elio” could confidently guess ETS, based on the “for short” in the clue. LACTIC acid can be assumed with the right few crossing letters, even if one doesn’t know anything about making sourdough bread. 

Experience also helps you to realize that “salsa”
Isn’t necessarily a condiment. It also may lead you to the intended meaning of [Inner tube?]. 

There are others, but I’m running out of characters. All in all, a decent Sat. challenge, requiring at least some degree of lateral thinking.

Philip GLASS (known for the piano not the flute, so nice Mr. Ect in that clue) may not be your cup of tea, but his piece written for the emoji keyboard always brings me to 😢.

(Maybe Bill from Detroit can find the link 😜)

I got to the puzzle later than usual, even for this congenital night owl. It was a little chewy, but I managed to finish it in decent time. My usual MO is to read Rex Parker's blog before Wordplay and the comments. But I was so curious about Moe Berg, who I had never heard of, that I went to Wikipedia first and read the entire lengthy entry. Wow, what an interesting character! And then to Rex Parker's blog, where he posted an abbreviated version of the story. Just my luck.

On my first pass I had exactly three entries, and they were fairly short. Little by little it all worked out. Got held up in the SE with BLUEY and DIEGO LUNA, a near Natick for me, but a couple of guesses on my non-erasable paper copy later proved were correct. Nice to have a challenge for a change.

This went very quickly for a Saturday for me. I thought there were an usually high number of fact-based clues/answers, many of which were in my wheelhouse.  
Any puzzle with Jennifer EGAN in it is okay by me, and she anchored the east side for me. Every time I wanted to enter something over there, I would look at those 4 letters and think, I absolutely know they are right, what will work with them? Same for THE KISS.
I normally give the Kardashians a WIDE BERTH, but I do have a hard time imagining them calling each other SIS.
I JUST WORK HERE was fun, and I also liked the J crossings.
So, thank you for an enjoyable puzzle, Mr Judge.

Like everyone else here, I know DIEGO LUNA from "Dirty Dancing: Havana Nights", universally recognized as being superior to "Dirty Dancing".

Who can honestly say they didn't tear up when his character says, "Nobody puts Baby in cryopreservation"?

Being a plodding solver (something like an hour and fifteen minutes for this one) has its advantages.

I thoroughly enjoyed this. My gold star was in question from the moment I read the 1-Across clue to the moments when I backspaced over an X and hit R to get EROTIC, after 15 minutes of flyspecking (I think that's an accurate use of the lingo?).

In between I appreciated the construction and cluing, got the satisfaction that comes from sticking-to-it, and learned a little.

Note the absolute minimum of glue and crosswordese in the grid.  ETS, EBB, OPS and that's about it? MES maybe?  No OREO's or ERA's or EON's? No "made up words"? In a wide open grid like this?  That's an accomplishment.

One to savor!

Good Saturday puzzle - just a bit above my pay grade. More than a couple of completely unfamiliar answers (e.g. EMOJIKEYBOARD, JOJOBAOIL, AFORTIORI), but also other things that I couldn't quite work out. No big deal - that's just me. And of course a puzzle find. A Sunday from December 4, 2011 by Kelsey Blakly with the title: "Swapping Partners." Some theme answers in that one: DIARYQUEEN PRISONGRAB INTERNALANGEL MARITALARTS ROUGEELEPHANT DEVILSLIAR TROTREFORM Here's that link: https://www.xwordinfo.com/Crossword?date=12/4/2011&h=55d See you tomorrow. ....

Well "I'm not the world's most physical guy" and just a regular old puzzler, but I slogged my way through this tough but enjoyable Saturday puzzle with some definitely needed look ups (afortiori and Diego Luna) to help me through. And now I am hearing L-L-L Lola!  (Great song worm to have in my head for the weekend!). Thanks.
Ahhh term limits… if only

Solved without assistance, but this one really knocked me around. Every portion of the grid pummeled me. Took over an hour before the last box fell.

What a fabulous puzzle! Cleverly clued, full of quirky entries, and loaded with uncommon letters. Only Z failed to appear today. Mr. Judge is fast becoming a favorite constructor of mine.

I'm guessing others found this much easier than I did; felt like nothing clicked today, and I had to work for every entry---exactly the Saturday experience I want.

Please, more like this one.
Perfect level of difficulty for me.  Very fun to solve.  Felt like I wouldn’t get there a few times.  Had to put it down and come back to it.

Ahhh. Just a little higher and to the left, please.
Looks like the gator in the column photo is enjoying his back rub.

Chewy Saturday puzzle, one of those where I had to break and come back a few times.

5A/7D, the LOTR extra and result of labor --- was that orc and child? Nope, ELF and FRUIT.

43D, is the flute considered a brASS instrument? Oh, he's referring to a wine GLASS.

36D, why isn't it a CREam EGG? Okay, CREME, whatever. I almost never got a cream egg at Easter, the good ones were expensive and the cheap ones were just candy-corn-like sugar bombs.

30A, touch, smart, EMOJI keyboard.

34A, tropic, exotic, EROTIC heat.

Lotsa SLY cluing here. Thanks, Ryan.

This puzzle was a beauty. Smooth, quick and somewhat challenging. I did have to look up Diego Luna to goose the SE corner (screeching halt there) because there were a few things my brain either did not know or could not grok.

I am giving up.  The NW corner has me on the ropes, and I just do not have extra time today (or indeed for the next 3 weeks, it looks like.)
I wanted SCORE for the conductor, KAL EL for "Super" man, REI was correct, I'm sure, but above that...a massive mess.  INRE? Or ASTO?  I even tried OXFAM for the Kenyan NGO.  I don't dare slap my head after I click on the Reveal.
Ryan Judge, nail my old white head on your trophy wall and smirk at it often!  Ya got me!

Late to the party, but isn't a sitar held at a 45 degree angle ACROSS the body and not FROM the body?

I hold my clarinet and my recorders at a 45 degree angle from the body. 

If you search "how to hold a sitar" all the images show a sitar held across the body, not from it. There are parts of the sitar, especially long ones, that extend at 45 degrees from the body, but the instruments themselves are not held from the body.

Happy to be corrected about this.

This was the hardest Saturday puzzle I've ever solved. It took nearly 2 hours (I had to look up the last name of the Andor actor). In the SE corner I was stuck on TONGS for "Grabber" and it took me a while to untangle. The NW corner was almost impossible. I had a lot of incorrect words initially: INRE instead of ASTO and KANJI instead of EMOJI. That took a half-hour to figure out. A real challenge but satisfying to finish.
Help wanted.
Very hard but fair.

Have been doing a bunch of Saturday puzzles from the archive lately, but this one reminded me that I have a ways to go. 
Also, niche comment: I didn’t recall DIEGO LUNA’s name b/c to me he is a poor imitation of Pedro Pascal! See: The Mandalorian > Andor

Lots of real estate in 33D.  Will cheating on DIEGO LUNA break this puzzle open for me?  Not a chance.

I can cheat on the top answer on the left on Rex without seeing any other answers.  Will that break the puzzle open for me?  Not a chance.  Though it does give me EXCUSE ME, a perfectly dreadful and completely unrelated answer to "Um...what?!"  If there's anything I hate more than this kind of loose, sloppy cluing, it's video game characters in whatever color you like; races of supernatural spirits; animated dogs; "super" men; and "Elio", whatever that is.

Smartphone features also flummox me.  I don't know where the EMOJI KEYBOARD resides, I've never used it, and if I did know where it was, I still wouldn't use it.  All those scrunched-up faces look exactly the same to me, and if you think you're sending me a message by sending me an EMOJI, what you're really sending me is a great big "Huh?"  Or rather a teensy-tiny "Huh?"

Puzzles like this make me feel both dumb and old -- as though I'm inhabiting an entirely different universe than the constructor.  Which I probably am.  I do like my world SO much better though!  Is there anyone else here who feels this way?  ANYONE AT ALL?  Please, please let me know if you do.  You'll make me feel so much less alone, outmoded and obsolete.

Very happy to see a challenging Saturday puzzle today! OXFAM crossing with AFORTIORI nearly ruined me, but I eventually plugged that F in and heard the happy music. And I learned two new words out of the deal!

Ent before ELF eDS before MDS canOlA before JOJOBA OIL absolutely nothing before DIEGO LUNA and, of course, Médecins Sans Frontières before OXFAM But, thank you, Kinks for your EROTIC LOLA [L-O-L-A] and her "dark brown voice". https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=6CzrYXcXweI Good, tough puzzle. Just what I like on a Saturday! Thanks, Ryan Judge. Keep 'em coming!

I'm surprised so many found this one so easy. It was an average-buster for me. 

In particular, the NW. 'Join [up]', 'mon amis', Latin, African NGOs, some videogame guy...yikes.

Poor clue for EXCUSE ME imo.

I did know JOJOBA OIL right away; there are many abandoned old jojoba farms around the southern Mojave desert.

A Saturday Saturday from where I sit

This one had a satisfying bit of bite, though I finished it in somewhat less than my average time.

Favorite clue was for EXCUSE ME?? I can just hear it, in a very particular tone of voice. Usually by a woman. Maybe more often in the South? I see the incredulous expression, with or without a touch of humor, depending on the situation.

It is one of my favorite reactions, suitable for a public scolding of the stranger who trod on your toes on the bus, or a private conversation with a friend who just said something monumentally uh, (fill in the blank with a non-emu-proof word).

Thanks, Ryan, for this very, very tough workout that I got without even heading to the gym today.😰😅

I've always found our local Trader Joe's employees to have the best customer service and also to be the most friendly among our area markets. I'm now adding erudite to that description. Well done -- fabulous construction feat!

I reckon there’s a battle behind the scenes between the puzzle editorial team and the app team.

The app team is no doubt focused on downloads, daily users, retention, session length. 

The puzzle team is focused on putting out great puzzles and maintaining legacy as the standard bearer for crosswords.

On Fridays and Saturdays those goals clearly don’t line up. The puzzle team can’t alienate the core and dishonor the legacy by allowing Wednesday level puzzles every weekend.  Meanwhile the app team can’t abide watching average session length plummet and new users churn.

It seems to me like they’ve landed on a compromise. Make one section genuinely difficult and let the rest of the puzzle play pretty smoothly.

That’s certainly been my experience the last few months. Consistently it seems I get hung up in one corner, while the rest of the puzzle is fairly breezy.

Makes sense - gives newer solvers enough footholds to keep battling, while leaving the pros with a little meat to chew on. 

The NW felt like that today.

Had to look up AFORTIORI. I had ExOTIC crossing. 
"Super man?" Is a great clue though. Beat my average. I enjoyed this one.

One more puzzle find - a Sunday from October 16, 1994 by Raymond Hamel with the title "Out of their element." Some theme answers, all straightforwardly clued: FLYINTHEOINTMENT CATCHERINTHERYE PIEINTHESKY BATSINTHEBELFRY BULLINACHINASHOP CASTLEINTHEAIR DOGINTHEMANGER Here's that link: https://www.xwordinfo.com/Crossword?date=10/16/1994&h=36d ....
Beautiful puzzle. The kind where you don’t get anything until you do. The kind that makes you believe in the sanctity of Xword again. So tremendous.
I gave up last night with over ¾ of the puzzle done. This morning it all came togather in about 5 minutes. I started working these puzzles around 1990 and I'm still amazed at how that happens.

I started reading the comments from newest to oldest today, not what I normally do. I wanted to get a sense of what people who solve later in the day thought about this one. 

For me, this was a return to the days of yore. After my first pass I had maybe three answers filled in. Finally, a real Saturday puzzle that’s not going to feel like a walk in the park! Mr. Judge is going to make me work for it. Great! 

Even the second and third passes did not yield a great deal. The answers came one by one, very slowly. I really liked all of them, even the shorter “fill”. Not answers that I see in a puzzle very often. 

But what really made the difference for me here were the clues. Many were very short and ambiguous. Is that a verb or a noun? And which meaning of the word? 

That’s what made it a Saturday puzzle for me. A real challenge to get to the end, and enough for me to have a little bit of doubt whether I could. 

I hope a lot of you got the same sense of accomplishment that I did from this one. Thank you, Ryan Judge.

What fun! Several clues really had me scratching my head, and there were several "Eureka!" moments, e.g. 3D & 13A. Overall, the difficulty was perfect; I surprised myself by solving in 10 minutes less than my Saturday average, but I felt like I was getting a real mental workout the whole time. Thank you, Mr. Judge!

The SE corner was the last to fill for me. I agree with Wordplay. This was a hard puzzle to do, but an easier one in retrospect. 
I won't jinx my streak by sharing a number, but I'm always relieved when Saturday's puzzle is done.
OK, I'm confused. Why is the answer for "line holders" RODS?
First pass through -- "I am doomed."  Second pass -- "Hmmm..." Third pass through -- "By jove, I think I've got it!"  Thanks, Ryan Judge!    P.S. "Left" was one heck of a clue!
Fast and fun

I loved this! Slow going with lots of erasing and changing, but lots of ahas and smiles along the way. I got the whole thing done with no lookups, which I can't often say about Saturdays, but it made me earn every letter. What a fun puzzle!

Not my cup of tea. Fair for a Saturday but I just couldn’t get on the constructors wavelength (if there even was one). 

To me, very vague clueing, with obscure, rarely used answers made this puzzle a slog. In addition, there were hardly any gimmes to help you in the crosses.

Very chewy. Like a disgusting piece of liver. Where the Hauk Tuah girl when you need her?

Rating:
5/10
What a great Saturday challenge..best in a long time. Thanks, Ryan, for a delightful game! No term limits for your puzzles.
Didn't care for the FAE/FIT or OXFAM entries. But loved seeing QBERT and MARIO hanging out in the same puzzle, even though it took me waaay too long to figure out that "Super man?" clue. All too
Loved this one. Extra points for TERMLIMIT 😉😉
Got really mixed up thinking that a flute was a brASS instrument. Once I got GLASS, for some reason I thought her name was LOLa...
Took me waaay longer than usual. Thanks Ryan!

Went fast for me (about half my average Saturday time), so personally I would have preferred this on a Friday to save space for a stiffer challenge. But I won’t complain too much — looks like others found it harder, and even to me it felt like it unfolded smoothly as I moved on the constructor’s wavelength, rather than like it gave no resistance (as has been the case too often lately).

Thanks Ryan for a quick Saturday spin.

Well this took me a reasonable amount of time, 30’+ but I didn’t do any word checks. I’m patient because am sitting here while they check up on my 
car maintenance. Glad to know I can still refrain from checking if there are no distractions! At first I wasn’t sure but like somebody said “I just kept getting more downs.”
Stubbornly gutted this one out.  Fixing nAE/neT/TeVO took longer than filling in the rest of the grid (which was already over my average time).
Mike, another home row, I mean home run.
Great puzzle, gave me a good stretch with the maybes! Thanks!

I completed the puzzle on my lunch break at work, in just under x minutes, which felt like it should be a personal best, although Statistics says my Saturday best was x/2. How can that be? I don't think I can even type that fast. But back to today's--was it because it was easy, or was it because my blood pressure was so high after a stressful morning shift, that I flew? This goes out to JohnWM in NB--a little Philip Glass, from when he still sounded like Philip Glass, the minimalist, and not like Philip Glass, the capitalist: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=K6K0u2ThQX8&list=RDK6K0u2ThQX8&start_radio=1 (I did not know this piece at all.)

I've been thinking... a dangerous past time, I know! I posted last night about my solve experience, which was overall quite a bit faster and smoother than my average for Saturday--except that I got stuck on the F in OXFAM and AFORTMORI. I had not heard of the former before and Latin is not my forte... 

Speaking of forte, it is indeed a word I know.  And I now feel like I could have guessed at the F based on it. Hmm. 

I stared at that crossing for so long after completing everything else in what may well have been record time....  I don't choose to run the alphabet on my KEYBOARD--EMOJI or otherwise--though I did run it in my brain. At the time, absolutely nothing seemed right. 

So, finally, I picked what seemed the least wrong to me at the moment but it was, of course, wrong, so that was that.  But now I feel like what the F was wrong with me!?  

Wish I would have pondered it more. I think forte would have done the trick for me. 

Lesson learned! Last night I wrote that I thought I would maybe remember OXFAM in the future, but probably not the Latin legal term, now I think I'll remember both. 

So all's well. ☺️

3 down; 13 across COME ON! Beyond the beyonds! And for the long one regarding "above my pay grade," I swear, I typed in PETE BUTTIGIEG because of his famous remark that our Creator made him gay but it was above his pay grade to know why. LOL. That expression always makes me think of Secy. Pete.

I'm not sure how I came up with OXFAM, but I confused it with the charity Omaze. The reason I knew Omaze was because of this incredible skit by Bryan Cranston and Aaron Paul. https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=IWeVx9zjN7I It an hilarious example of how they both excel at comedic timing. And I love how they give the big moment and the big line to a non-star.

Sometimes when I'm struggling in a section that the Wordplay column does not have many revelers for (the NW corner for me today), I glance at the comments for extra help ... and I always *dread* looking.

It seems like the comments are so often full of solvers bashing the constructors or the editors, complaining how easy the puzzle was for a whatever day, complaining about too many gimmes, not enough gimmes, complaining about the cluing, lamenting the "declining quality" of the Times crossword, and attacking each other. Thankfully today was not like that. Hopefully the amount of complaints and hostility  will decrease in the future so people don't dread peeking in here
That was extra difficult for me. Thank you.

Odd about this puzzle: it was challenging and took quite a while, but didn’t feel enjoyable, for some reason.  In contrast, later today I also did the 7/18/98 NYT and 7/18/26 Saturday Stumper crosswords, and did like both of them.

poem made from words in today’s puzzle

a/ above the black mountains
up up up above the swinging roots
up in the brilliant brilliant
d/ a butterfly
a/ just up

Challenging but also lots of fun, and I learned some new things for sure. I watched Andor and other movies he was in, so I remembered Diego Luna. I could be wrong, but I have a feeling he was a guest host on Jimmy Kimmel's show fairly recently too. AFORTIORI was a tough one too but I got it eventually with help from crosses. Could have asked my husband the former lawyer to help on that one, but he was busy watching the World Cup. I do often ask him for help on sports clues. And math clues too. He's a keeper and we will be celebrating our 55th anniversary soon.
I was going to ask for an explanation from the community about GRUBS at 43 across, which I eventually got from crosses, but looking at the clue again just now, it suddenly came to me that DIGS was a noun and not a verb :-)
Thanks for a great Saturday puzzle, Ryan Judge.

From my experience with Trader Joe’s from 2012, at its locations in Brookfield WI and South Bend IN, their employees are consistently very helpful, and anyone you’d ask who’s wearing the name tag would drop everything to help you find what you’re looking for, so I JUST WORK HERE seems like maybe the constructor doesn’t fit that culture well?

After somehow missing a flight I gave myself plenty of time to catch (goodbye, $900) I was at least pleased to make my way through this puzzle while I wait for the next one. (Flight, not puzzle.)

Wasn’t sure I’d finish initially. Spent a good amount of time wondering what sort of JOKE BOARD smartphones had, and why I’d never noticed the feature.
Overtook is HOARDED? How?
I have finally ascended from the larval commenter lambasting the esoterica in the puzzle to a beautiful crosswording moth who consumes the puzzle without hints and complains that it was too easy.

userLocation: Hong Kong sometimes; Portland this month
commentBody:
Wow that was tough!  But finished on first attempt and with no lookups.  Set it aside and came back to it several times.  And solve time was long as it usually is for Saturdays.  

Very enjoyable to me.  Thanks!!

Mark

@Barry Ancona

But I’d be surprised if there was a bunch of “too easy” on this one. I wouldn’t call today ‘hard’. But it doesn’t seem like a personal best kinda of puzzle, at least to me. But again we’ll see.

@SP

It was a weird puzzle for me. I found it quite challenging but ultimately I solved it in typical Saturday time (just needing to check one square against the column). Beating a Saturday usually feels nice but today it just didn't - for a reason I can't quite place I did not find the solve particularly enjoyable. It was a decent grid and I have nothing critical to say about it, really (other than the fact I found the clue for TOMB a stretch, as explained elsewhere on the board today), but I just did not get any sort of kick out of it...

@Andrzej

See my comment about TOMB on your thread

@Francis

Yes today we were on the same page in terms of difficulty (although though sounds like I enjoyed it more)

And remember the "early returns" are always going to be the fast solvers.

@Francis

I did find today the e word, except for the one crossing that did me in...  The Nairobi NGOs crossing Latin, but yesterday, meaning Friday, I found it was rather difficult for me and yet the great majority thought it was very easy. 🤷‍♀️

I started with VIOLA for the 45° instrument, but fortunately I eventually remembered the author's name and have read some of her books. 

Maybe I'm not into LOTR enough, or maybe it's because I think that Orlando Bloom made an awfully a rather fine looking elf that I only thought of ELF.

SIS came completely on the crossings and I didn't really notice it.

I dunno, obviously you have to know some things, learn the language of crossword, and be reasonably well-informed about our world, but I guess I'm trying to say I just don't always think it's about being smart or the opposite.  I do think I learned a lot more about solving from the ones that were still considered harder than I do from the Great Easening (royalties to B in NY)

I think unless you're a really experienced solver, it comes down to a lot of wheelhouse and you know whether or not you think Orlando Bloom is a super hot elf...

@SP

I'm not really in the mood to enjoy anything at the moment.

@Francis

Securing your place in line by putting your quarter on the screen bezel. 

Good times

@Weak

:-)  No.  Strange as it sounds, I was in grad school before even Pong came out.

@Weak I just finished reading this article: https://www.centraloregondaily.com/destination-oregon/next-level-pinball-museum-hillsboro/article_ca82c3c6-f0b5-4655-b296-f57a25e89c6c.html It looks like a place you'd enjoy! (I might also, if it's true it's not all pinball and they have Galaga...)

@Francis

I think the Great Easening is the preferred PC term...

@Matt

Yes, agreed, but B from NY has demanded royalties each time it's used. I think That's fair.  ☺️

@Francis
Even the smartest scientist will say that smarts do not succeed without experience. My experience has been that sometimes a sock works its way into a trouser leg and resides there until one is walking down the hall at the office or walking up to give a presentation. I have more than once found a missing sock of mine on the floor in the middle of a hallway. So, unless one sock has completely become lint, it probably left the dryer in trou .

@HeathieJ

He needs to post his Venmo deets, then.

@HeathieJ

Cate Blanchett and Morfydd Clark routinely rattle my sword.

@Matt

Morfydd Clark played Galadriel in the TV series?

Yeah, I often found myself staring a bit too longingly at her, for a man my age.

For some reason, though, I regard Cate Blanchett as a great, entertaining actress, but I don't think of teenager things when I see her.

@Francis

She did, sir.  Rose to prominence in her bravura horror performance in 2019's Saint Maude.  Highly recommended film just for her star turn, even if you're not a big horror fan.

"A man my age..."

Pshaw, man!

@Francis

Socks don’t vanish into thin air, and neither is there a secret compartment in the dryer where they go to hide. 

The missing sock is either:

—still in the hamper,
—behind or under the hamper (particularly difficult of the hamper is part of a cabinet),
—still in the washer,
—still in the dryer,
—somewhere on the bedroom floor or on the dresser, never having been put into the hamper,
—somewhere on the floor en route to being put away,
—inside an article of clean laundry.

Still in the washer is the worst, because now you have to run the dryer for one sock, so another load, or hang the one wet sock over the shower for the next 24 hours. 

Ask me how I know.

@Francis
We had a dog who would eat any unattended sock, which can be fatal if untreated. To avoid panic-driven searches for odd socks, I washed them together in a laundry bag. The dog is gone now, and so is the laundry bag. Our socks have reverted to their old ways.

I should have added that a way to help avoid the occurrence of some lost socks is then to turn pants and shirts inside out before tossing them into the washer and dryer. Static cling is part of the problem as well.

@Francis
It's because the dryer is a portal to a warp in the fabric of space time. (Sorry but I don't remember who came up with this idea and I refuse to ask AI.)

@Steve L
Well, yes and no. The Laws of Conservation prevent entire human-handling-sized objects from disappearing.
But I believe they can transmogrify into other lost objects. Candidates include:
- wire hangers on the floor of your closet. Haven't you noticed a direct correlation between sock vanishments and hanger apparitions?
- pen caps with no bottoms and pen bottoms with no caps. This possibility is more challenging as it requires not only transmogrification but also transportation. Socks and hangers are at least both in the clothing zone. But pens and the unmatched caps are in another area of the home entirely.
- raccoons. Least likely, I admit, as it requires not only transmogrification and transportation but also animation. But domestic aggravation levels for both socks and raccoons are congruent; both engage in active concealment and evade capture; and both exhibit a preliminary engaging softness that misleads us into thinking that they can't possibly wreak the havoc that they will cause if ignored.

@Mike

There no Escape from your puns, they are out of Control!

@Mike Here’s a tip for those who want flowing prose (but they really have to faucet): attach a garden hose to your typewriter, and you’ll soon find your writing’s qwerty 
🤪

@SP
Maybe he can shift into another space.

@Mike
Despite our options, we return to your puns and the shifting trail of replies all day long. Thank you for sharing your space.
@Lpr oops I looked and it was under my average for Saturday which is about 27 min

@Lpr

Back in the day, as her birthday approached, my wife expressed interest in having two things:  a small garage fridge, and a TiVo. So dutifully, I bought her both for her birthday. 

A hot minute later, Verizon Fios started offering DVR services at a much better rate, and shortly thereafter, my mother-in-law moved from a full-sized house to a senior apartment, and her full-sized garage fridge became available to us, and we took it. 

So we were left with a TiVo we didn’t need or use, and as third fridge that sat unplugged in the garage until we moved, about ten years later.

@Lpr

Back in the day, as her birthday approached, my wife expressed interest in having two things:  a small garage fridge, and a TiVo. So dutifully, I bought her both for her birthday. 

A hot minute later, Verizon Fios started offering DVR services at a much better rate, and shortly thereafter, my mother-in-law moved from a full-sized house to a senior apartment, and her full-sized garage fridge became available to us, and we took it. 

So we were left with a TiVo we didn’t need or use, and as third fridge that sat unplugged in the garage until we moved, about ten years later.

@lucky13

I don't think the founding fathers thought that being an elected official was a valid lifetime occupation.  The two term limit was obeyed through the first 31 presidents even without it being a constitutional limitation.

I'd be curious to know how long the average congressman and senator served from about 1800 to 1950.  Did they, too, hang onto office as if it were a life jacket in a hurricane?  

Maybe it just took this long for the graft and corruption in government to grow to the point that it was preferable to the graft and corruption of the business world?

@Francis

My close friend, ChatGPT answered your question "During most of the nineteenth century, a typical representative had served only one term or slightly more, while a senator had generally served less than one full six-year term. By 1950, members of both chambers averaged roughly six to seven years of previous service."
@lucky13 It pays to be in office. Check out the Nancy Pelosi Stock Tracker account on X sometime.
@Weak You’re too generous with your accent in creme. Pretty sure it’s spelled that way for legal reasons because they don’t contain any dairy.

@Rachel
They may not contain any cream, i.e., the full-fat product of milk, but they certainly contain dairy. Does the US have a separate formulation?
Sam Lyons — it was the same SE region that took me about as long as the rest of the puzzle had already taken.

@Francis

Long ago, my cousins from California stayed with our family for a day or two.  They wore mittens. To the mall. It was August! I’ve never been able to erase that memory.  Gah!

@Francis Summers are getting hotter here, no question. As first a Minnesotan with Scandinavian genes and now a longtime Montanan, I prefer northern places with cooler temps. We’re in the 90s here now on a daily basis and it feels like living on the surface of the sun.

@Aaron Teasdale I've got to the point where I can usually get most of a Saturday puzzle without help. But from time to time I just can't get going and reveal a bunch of stuff. At my age I don't let pastimes like this bother me if I'm not perfect – life's too short.

@dutchiris

Right there with you.

I was gasping until IJUSTWORKHERE finally dawned on me.  And it still wasn't until I realized the 30A phone feature was for the year 2011, not currently, that I finally felt (mostly) comfortable.  

Even then, I think if I hadn't lucked out and didn't need to flyspec, I probably would have given up.

@dutchiris
AFORTIORI is my standard example of how my parochial school education prepared me for law school. I had all the legal concepts; I just had to remember to sub in the Latin for the Hebrew or Aramaic.

@Ιασων
I tend to associate BONGO drums with hippies, not salsa. I wanted some Latin music at my wedding, so I added a couple of live conga players to the DJ package. But I guess BONGOS are used in salsa, too.
@Andrzej fishing rod holds the fishing  line. Equally confused about grubs though. I was trying to make grips work there.

@Andrzej

One at a time:

1.  AFORTIORI is a gimme for us philosophy/logic geeks, too.  Don't just have to chase after vehicles with sirens to know it.

2.  Some TOMBs contain families.  So center works.

3.  Grub:  (ambitransitive) To dig; to dig up by the roots; to root out by digging; often followed by up. 
to grub up trees, rushes, or sedge

It's just an obscurer use of a common word.

4.  Fishing RODS hold fishing line(s).

I got you, bro.
@Andrzej A fishing rod holds a line.
@Andrzej  Aw. Were you misdirected? That mean, mean constructor.

@Hugh

Why the snark? When you try to diss somebody, at least put some effort into it rather than demonstrate poor reading comprehension. I literally said I solved the clue without any issue, so no, I was not misdirected, at all. My point was I thought the tomb clue was a stretch rather than misdirection 🤷🏽

In olden times a tomb could be found on a family estate with no other dead people around.  No cemetery needed. 

Hence, a “dead center.”

A rod is a fishing rod, absotively. 

Mic drop.

@Matt

Point taken. Thanks for making me feel better about the clue.

@Andrzej

NO!

Fight me!

Fight me now!!

@Matt

I'm not into elder abuse.

@Andrzej

Yer just yeller.

@Matt

You lost me. Do remember I'm not a native speaker of English.
Was it about being yellow as in chickening out?

@Andrzej

Yawp.

@Francis

Do. Or do not. There is no try.

@Andrzej
If I had been the constructor or editor, I might have clued GRUBS as "cadge a cigarette." But maybe that's old slang.

@Times Rita

Maybe, Rita...Hayworth?

@Andrzej

Do you mean because you think of TOMB as more for a single person rather than a congregation of dead people? Because it struck me that way just for a second, but then I would think all that time you and I spend in video games raiding a single tomb with hundreds of skeletons whacking at us would have made it seem more appropriate.

@SP

Look up for my reply to Matt - I comment on this there.

@SP

As for your gaming example:

1. When solving I generally use daily life as a frame of reference, so tombs evoke municipal graveyards rather than ancient catacombs

2. And as for catacombs: I think of them as a place ("dead center", say) with many individual tombs (which individually are not "dead centers"). I mean, when my wife and I visited the early Christian catacombs in Naples, we weren't inside a tomb, as such, but rather in a man-made cave with hundreds of tombs cut into the walls.

Anyway, I got the clue, I just didn't like it, and ultimately that's a subjective thing.

@Andrzej

Not usually.

It's stereotypic deep South vernacular for "You're just yellow."

Often heard in older westerns.

"Yellow" meaning cowardly.
@Whoa Nellie nicely done 👏🏼👏🏼👏🏼

@RobcoThere are Oxfam charity shops (thrift stores?) on every high street in the UK so that was a gimme once the 'x' was revealed. They have specific outlets just to sell books that tend to be really good.
I take it these are less common in the US?

@Robco, you don't need to be a lawyer to get A FORTIORI if all you are missing is the F; you just need a tiny bit of etymology: "force" comes from Latin "fortis", and the clue is about "strong" reasoning, so it makes sense. (I hadn't heard the legal term, but it felt fair enough after a few crosses.)

@Eel

There are “charity shops” run by Goodwill in the US, but OXFAM doesn’t have them here. It’s just an NGO we might occasionally hear about on the news.

@Eel I've never heard of Oxfam shops in the US. If we believe Google's AI summary, there are none: "In the U.S., the concept of the "charity shop" is typically fulfilled by organizations like Goodwill, The Salvation Army, Habitat for Humanity ReStore, or local independent thrift stores run by religious or community groups. Unlike Oxfam GB, Oxfam America does not utilize a storefront-based fundraising model."

@Eel
Not just "less common"--practically invisible/unknown.
I only knew it from reading British novelists, but I took it OUT of my grid because I couldn't get it to work with other entries.
I have had a really bad day; should've walked away for a while.

@Bob
'If'
@Matt I just learned I never had that lyric right either.

@Nik

I suggest community hands-up posts with wrong lyrics...
@Matt In a background voice?

@Petrol

@Petrol

Check out Clair Obscur: Expedition 33 for another great example of this. If you don't want to play the game, look up the music on YouTube. It's fantastic.

@Classic Hip-Hop Fan
Verizon would not support the card to interface with the TiVo. I hate their DVR. I loved my TiVo!
@David Johnson I had the same experience!

@David Johnson --- northwest corner for me too. Same problem with 1D. xwstats.com says "Average" difficultly probably thanks to my time spent in that corner more than making up for all of you that breezed through the grid. 

Having inre instead of ASTO at 23A did me no favors --- I felt pretty sure about that one. That led me to be convinced the Latin must be ...prIORI. [Super Man?] I could only think of someone who takes care of an apartment building. Finally the crossword muse gave me EXCUSEME and things fell, albeit still slowly, into place.

@jas

My solve in the NW exactly

@Helen Wright
I was surprised that people didn't know OXFAM. But then again, I spent a lot of time in London in 1978 and ran across them there. And then once you contribute to one charity here you get mail from all the others.  I didn't know they were Nairobi-based, but, as another poster said, once you see that X from the cross, the lightbulb goes on.

Lewis,
Lovely answers indeed. Too few SLY clues (IMO).

@Barry Ancona -- I may have had your reaction, but my brain was not operating on all cylinders due to a middle-of-the-night awake stretch involving nearby loud fireworks, and, in our bed, as a result, a frantic dog.  I know my brain was slow because it took me 10 minutes to remember the gimme REI!  Anyway, on whatever level I was at, I really enjoyed this puzzle.

Lewis,
After a masterful but easy-to-solve Friday puzzle, I was hoping for a Saturday challenge. I was disappointed to not be challenged. That's not on Ryan Judge, who presented an excellent grid ("lovely answers indeed"). I'm sure you would have enjoyed the answers solving in half the time it took you. You had a non-puzzle challenge for this one.
@Lewis. And a beautiful grid too.
@CCNY Could people stop using disability words to describe other situations?
Interesting, Ms. Spaight. I have some hearing loss, but I've never heard the term "deaf ears" used to refer to a disability.

@Ms. Billie M. Spaight
"Falling on deaf ears" is a tried-and-true expression. I have some hearing loss and it doesn't bother me one bit. Does every disability have to have a euphemism?

@Times Rita
This could have been stated in a kinder manner.  You do not (nor do I) know why this gives Ms. Spaight pain, but why disparage her obvious distress?

@Katie
I doubt it has that effect--making it "more difficult" for "disabled people" to get respect.  

You, yourself, put  'disabled' before "people."  Is she to blame for that?  And am I making it "much more difficult" by trying to explain?  Or am I speaking based on decades experience?  
Can't we all just get along?

Yikes. 
So much of my life includes
hearing impaired friends and family,
that I learned ASL as a child.

My children did the same.

I was referring to the expression.

If you are hearing impaired, deaf, 
or feel hurt because the expression
is painful to someone you care about,
I understand your plea.

In any case, please know
that not all references
are derogatory or imply
a lack of sympathy.

That makes sense, but in such a contorted way that the cluing is the weaker for it. Replacing from with across - would that have been, in the editors' eyes, a lessening of the clue's difficulty level?

@Charles Peterson, "From" implies that the instrument extends from the body, no matter what position or shape of the body, at a 45 degree angle. "From the body to the left" also suggests that it extends from a starting point of the body out to the left. Despite Barry's suggestion, which is probably what was meant by the clue, I think it's badly clued and "across" would have solved the problem, but potentially made the cluing less Saturday in difficulty.

@john ezra

I don't think the cluing is weaker. It's more tricksy, as it should be on a Saturday.

@Nancy

I get EXCUSEME, but Cincinnatians have this weird habit of saying “Please?” when they want something repeated.

@SP I used to live in Cincinnati and Germans, and the reason for this is really interesting. The “please” thing is a relic from German due to the sheer amount of German immigrants to the area, as in German it’s very common to say “please” (bitte) when you don’t hear someone. It can also be used as a “you’re welcome”! You learn something new every day :)

"Please" and "You're welcome" are identical in French, German, Greek, Hebrew, and a whole bunch of other languages. I begin to see a pattern...

@Charles Peterson

Ce n'est pas "mon amis." (It isn't "mon amis.")

C'est "mes amis." (It's "mes amis.")--"mes" is plural like "amis."

mon ami--one male friend of mine

mon amie--one female friend of mine

mes amis--a group of male friends of mine

mes amies--a group of female friends of mine

mes amis--a group of male and female friends of mine

Special note: because "ami" starts with a vowel, we use "mon" instead of the usual female form "ma"

There you go. For next time.

For extra credit. "La plume de ma tante est sur le bureau de mon oncle."--famous French saying

@Delg
¡Mira!
I've found that learning one or two interjections in any language can get you through entire conversations.
¿Verdad?
You won't understand anything but your interlocutor won't notice.
PS In French,  I recommend Donc and Hein.
PPS In any language, avoid simple yesses and noes. Otherwise, before you know it, you've agreed to pick up the tab or, worse yet, share the driving on his trip to Dusseldorf.

Before someone complains that you would not say, "Um...what?!" when someone stepped on your toes, that is true. The clue fits the private conversation version--along with its office variant, the "You may be my co-worker but did you really just make that offensive and unenlightened comment?!"

It is an expression that, all by itself, justifies the invention of the interrobang.
